title: practical english usage - Part 164
content:
This is a list of a few expressions which  often cause problems. For other and   expressions        _______________________ 212 prepositions         before     particular       words For details of the structures that are possible after a particular verb, noun or adjective, see a good dictionary.

I was interested to learn that my grandfather was Jewish.

I'm interested in learning more about my family.

(►105   for some examples).

Sometimes two structures are possible. There may be a difference of meaning We're travelling to enjoy ourselves.

- We're travelling for pleasure.

She was surprised to see what he had done.

- She was surprised at his mistake.

He asked to borrow some money.

- He asked for a loan.

In some cases we  drop the preposition and use an infinitive. Compare: not I'm not very good at cooking, (     ... good-at-to eeete.) not I don't like the idea of getting married, (     ... the idea of to get married.) not He insisted on being paid at once, (     He insisted on to be paid...) noun/adjective + preposition, we  usually use the -ing form of a following verb.

Prepositions are not normally used before infinitives in English. After verb/ 211  -ing    forms     and   infinitives see a good dictionary.

For the structures (with and without prepositions) that are possible after a particular verb, noun or adjective, not I'm worried about whether you're happy, (     I'm worried about if. ..) If does not normally follow prepositions; we  use whether (►  261) instead.

North or the South, (     ... according whether. ..) People's chances of getting Jobs vary according to whether they live in the (More natural than ... the question who's going to pay.) There's the question of who's going to pay.

The police questioned me about what I'd seen.

I'm worried about where she is. (     Ibn-werrierLwkere-she-iS:) In other cases it is unusual or impossible to leave out the preposition.

Look (at) what I've got.

- Look at this.

I've no idea (of) who owns it.

- I've no idea of the owner.

Prepositions               18 grammar •  213   prepositions after particular words and expressions consult a good dictionary.

notes here; for more complete information about usage with a particular word, American and British usage sometimes differ. There is only room for very brief cause difficulty to students of English. Alternatives are sometimes possible, and verb or adjective. Here are some of the most common combinations which It is not always easy to know which  preposition to use after a particular noun, a nd   expressions        ___________________ 213 prepositions         after    particular       words He would have died if they hadn’t got him  to the hospital in time.

in time (= with enough time to spare, before the last moment) Daniel wants the meeting to start exactly on time.

on time (= at the planned time, neither late nor early) It’s Mrs Ellis on the phone: she says it’s urgent.

Is there anything good on TV tonight?

on the radio; on TV; on the phone There’s a mistake on page 120.

on page 20, etc (     ln/at) Stop talking to me in that stupid voice.

Who’s the man in the funny hat over there?

in a suit, raincoat, shirt, skirt, hat, etc I like walking in the rain.

in the rain, snow, etc She looks much  younger in this photo.

in a picture, photo, etc (     on) Please fill in the form in ink.

in pen, pencil, ink, etc I think the film’s a bit weak at the end.

at the end (= at the point where something stops) In the end, I got a visa for Russia.

in the end (= finally, after a long time) In my opinion, she should have resigned earlier.

not                              or in . .. opinion (     aeeθrding to     after^) Try to see it from my point of view.

not                               or from . .. point of view (     aeeoFding to     after) My sister decided to go to America for several reasons.

Let’s take our time and go by boat.

by car/bike/bus/train/boat/plane/land/sea/air; on foot (but in the car, I’ve never read anything by Dickens.

a film (directed) by Orson Welles (     ef      tan) a book (written) by Dickens; a concerto (composed) by Mozart; grammar •  213   prepositions after particular words and expressions I'm not very clever at cooking.                       -> not clever at (     In) care: ►    419.
